title: Will the unemployment rate (U-3) be above 4.1% in June?

current.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 94%, modal YES) — staying out.
current.rationale_summary: The evidence suggests mixed signals regarding the U.S. labor market in early 2026. While headline jobs numbers indicate strength (e.g., 'Strong, Strong Jobs' in May), there are indications of underlying slack (e.g., 'May’s Headline Jobs Numbers Mask Underlying Labor Market Slack'). Regional data, such as Hawaii’s unemployment rate at 2.5% and Arizona’s softening labor market, suggest local variati
key_drivers: Mixed signals in headline jobs numbers vs. underlying labor market slack
Regional variations in unemployment and job gains
Potential underreporting of labor market slack
reference_classes: U-3 vs. U-6 Rates: Unemployment & Underemployment
Alternative Measures of Labor Underutilization in New York — 2025

history.rationale_summary: U-3 has held at 4.3% for April and May 2026; resolving NO requires a rare 0.2pp single-month drop against Iran-war and tariff headwinds and rising jobless claims.

history.lean_note: I lean YES (my prob 92%) but it isn't +EV at the ask — no value bet (betting the other side would contradict my forecast).
history.rationale_summary: May 2026 U-3 = 4.3%, in a 4.3-4.5% range since Jul 2025; no sign of a drop below 4.1% in June. Very likely above 4.1%.

history.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 93%, modal YES) — staying out.
history.rationale_summary: U-3 has printed 4.3-4.5% every month since mid-2025 (May 4.3%, June consensus 4.3-4.4%); a sub-4.1% print needs a multi-tenth downside surprise with no catalyst.

history.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 90%, modal YES) — staying out.
history.rationale_summary: The evidence suggests mixed signals regarding the U.S. labor market in May 2026. While some reports highlight strong job gains and stable unemployment rates, others indicate underlying labor market slack.
